does: This paper evaluates methods for extracting well-calibrated confidence scores from large language models fine-tuned with reinforcement learning from human feedback, focusing on RLHF-based systems like ChatGPT, GPT-4, and Claude. The core contribution is a systematic empirical study showing that verbalized confidences—where the model explicitly outputs confidence statements as text tokens—significantly outperform the model's conditional probabilities as reliability measures. Testing across three benchmarks (TriviaQA, SciQ, TruthfulQA), the authors demonstrate that verbalized confidences can reduce expected calibration error by approximately 50% relative to raw probability estimates, providing a practical strategy for obtaining trustworthy uncertainty quantification from modern instruction-tuned language models.
connects: This work builds on established research in confidence calibration and uncertainty quantification in machine learning, extending it to the RLHF setting. It responds directly to prior findings showing that while base language models maintain well-calibrated conditional probabilities, the RLHF fine-tuning process degrades calibration—a concern raised in recent studies of instruction-tuned models. The paper connects to broader literature on eliciting reliable confidence from neural networks and probing the properties of models trained with reinforcement learning. It also builds on the intuition that language models can explicitly reason about uncertainty when prompted to do so, leveraging their ability to generate natural language outputs as a mechanism for uncertainty quantification rather than relying solely on implicit probability estimates.
enables: This work enables the development of more trustworthy deployed language model systems by providing a practical technique for extracting reliable confidence scores without requiring architectural modifications or additional training. It opens avenues for research into why verbalization improves calibration and how this phenomenon generalizes across different model families and domains. The findings support future work on human-AI collaboration systems where models can appropriately defer to experts based on genuine uncertainty, improving safety and reliability in high-stakes applications. Additionally, this research points toward developing better human feedback training procedures that preserve or enhance calibration, and investigating whether other forms of explicit reasoning or decomposition can further improve uncertainty quantification in fine-tuned language models.
